planting.note: Start indoors well before the last frost; transplant only after soil has warmed.

directions: This heirloom Physalis produces small golden fruits inside papery, lantern-like husks with a sweet pineapple-vanilla flavor. Start seeds indoors six to eight weeks before your last frost and set out sprawling plants once nights stay warm. Fruits are ripe when the husks turn tan and drop naturally to the ground, so harvest from the soil below the plant. Let them cure a few days in their husks to develop full sweetness for fresh eating or preserves.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Hale's Best is a classic heirloom cantaloupe prized for exceptional sweetness, tender juicy flesh, and a small seed cavity that yields more edible fruit. Sow seeds in warm soil once frost has passed, giving vines several feet to sprawl. Water consistently while fruit develops, then ease off as melons near ripeness to concentrate sugars. Harvest at full slip, when the melon separates cleanly from the vine with a light pull.

planting.note: Direct sow after the soil warms and frost risk is gone.

directions: Hearts of Gold is a beloved heirloom cantaloupe with fragrant, honey-sweet orange flesh, usually maturing at two to three pounds. Sow directly into warm, well-drained soil in full sun once frost danger has passed. Keep moisture even during fruit set and growth, then reduce watering as the melons ripen to boost flavor. Harvest when fruit slips easily from the vine and gives off a strong sweet aroma.

planting.note: Sow directly after the soil is reliably warm; lima beans rot in cold ground.

directions: This compact heirloom butterbean, introduced in the late 1800s, is loved for its reliability, mild flavor, and easy sprouting. Sow seeds directly into warm soil, as lima beans germinate poorly in cold or wet ground. The bushy plants need no staking and produce flat pods of small, creamy beans. Pick pods when plump for shelling fresh, or leave them to dry on the plant for storage beans.

planting.note: Direct sow in blocks after frost for good wind pollination.

directions: Hickory King is a historic heirloom white dent corn with unusually large kernels, ideal for grits, hominy, masa, and cornmeal. Sow directly into warm soil in blocks of at least four short rows to ensure good pollination. Give plenty of nitrogen and steady moisture as the tall stalks develop. Let ears dry fully on the stalk before harvesting for grinding or long-term storage.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after the last frost when nights stay above 50F.

directions: This West Virginia heirloom produces huge one-to-two-pound beefsteak fruits with striking yellow-and-red marbled coloring and sweet, low-acid flavor. Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant once the soil warms. The indeterminate vines can reach five feet or more and need sturdy staking or caging. Support heavy fruit clusters and harvest when the marbled fruits give slightly to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Sow directly once frost is past and soil is warm.

directions: Honey Rock, an All-America Selections winner from 1933 also known as Sugar Rock, offers honey-sweet salmon-colored flesh and dependable performance. Direct sow seeds in warm, fertile soil in full sun after frost danger passes. Keep vines evenly watered during growth, then reduce moisture as fruit ripens to intensify sweetness. Harvest at full slip when the melon parts easily from the vine and smells fragrant.

planting.note: Best in hot-climate gardens; direct sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Honeydew produces sweet, refreshing melons with crisp pale-green flesh that thrive in hot summer gardens. Sow seeds directly in warm soil and full sun after all frost has passed, giving the long-season vines ample room. Provide steady water during fruit development and a long, warm growing season for the fruit to sweeten fully. Unlike cantaloupe, honeydew does not slip, so harvest when the rind turns creamy and the blossom end softens slightly.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or late summer for a fall crop; heat causes bolting.

directions: This heirloom crisphead forms compact, tight heads about eight inches across with firm, mild, refreshingly crunchy leaves. Sow in cool weather, either early spring or late summer, since heat triggers bolting and bitterness. Thin plants to give each head room to fill out and keep the soil consistently moist. Harvest when heads feel firm and full, cutting at the base in the cool part of the day.

planting.note: Direct sow; carrots resent transplanting. Keep seedbed moist until emergence.

directions: This AAS-winning heirloom yields long, tapered orange roots with fine-grained texture, outstanding sweetness, and thin, easy-to-peel skin. Sow seeds directly into loose, stone-free soil so the long roots can grow straight. Keep the seedbed consistently moist during the slow germination period, then thin seedlings to prevent crowding. Harvest once roots reach full size and sweeten further after a light frost.

planting.note: Sow in spring or late summer; cool weather mellows the bitterness.

directions: Despite its name, this is an Italian chicory with vivid burgundy stems and deeply lobed green leaves, grown for flavorful bitter greens. Sow directly in cool weather, spring or late summer, when the flavor is at its most balanced. Thin seedlings and keep soil moist to encourage tender leaves. Harvest young leaves for salads or let plants mature for cooking greens with a robust chicory bite.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; count back from Halloween for timing.

directions: This classic heirloom pumpkin has smooth orange skin, dependable size, and the perfect round shape for carving jack-o'-lanterns. Sow directly into warm, rich soil after frost, allowing the sprawling vines plenty of space. Feed and water consistently as fruit sets and swells, tucking a board or straw under each pumpkin to prevent rot. Harvest when rinds harden and turn deep orange, cutting with a length of stem attached for better keeping.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil; excels in hot, dry climates.

directions: This historic dwarf bush butterbean, introduced in 1888, is prized for heat tolerance, resilience, and dependable yields in hot, dry conditions. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, since lima beans will not germinate in cold ground. The compact bush plants need no support and produce speckled, buttery-textured beans. Harvest young pods for fresh shelling or let them dry fully on the plant for storage.

planting.note: Start indoors early; peppers need warm soil and nights above 55F to transplant.

directions: This classic heirloom produces thick-walled, three-to-four-inch fruits with moderate heat and robust flavor for stuffing, slicing, or grilling. Start seeds indoors eight weeks before the last frost, as peppers need a long warm season. Transplant only after nights stay reliably above 55 degrees and the soil has warmed. Pick fruits green for typical jalapeno flavor, or let them ripen red for extra sweetness and heat.

planting.note: Sow in blocks after frost; isolate from other corn to keep it true.

directions: This compact heirloom reaches about five feet tall, making it well suited to smaller gardens while producing two or three short, full ears per stalk. Sow directly into warm soil in blocks of several rows to ensure good wind pollination. Keep it isolated from sweet or dent corn so the popping quality stays true. Let ears dry fully on the stalk, then cure the tender, low-hull kernels further indoors before popping.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant in early spring; tolerates light frost.

directions: This historic heirloom, dating to the 1840s, forms distinctive pointed heads with sweet flavor and matures early on compact plants ideal for small gardens. Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant hardened seedlings in early spring. Cabbage tolerates light frost, so it can go out before tender crops. Keep soil evenly moist and harvest the conical heads when firm to avoid splitting.

planting.note: Needs a long, hot season; start indoors and transplant into warm soil.

directions: Also called Mexican turnip or yam bean, jicama is a warm-season vine grown for crisp, juicy tubers with a lightly sweet, nutty flavor. It needs a long, hot growing season, so start seeds indoors and transplant into warm soil, providing a trellis for the climbing vines. Only the swollen root is edible; the pods, leaves, and seeds are toxic and should not be eaten. Dig the tubers before the first frost, once they have had four to five months to bulk up.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil at the base of a trellis or poles.

directions: This time-tested heirloom snap bean produces heavy yields of long, smooth green pods that are stringless when picked young. Sow directly into warm soil at the base of sturdy poles or a trellis, since the vigorous vines climb six feet or more. Keep the plants evenly watered and pick pods frequently to keep them tender and productive. Regular harvesting encourages the vines to set new flushes of beans all season.

planting.note: Sow directly into warm soil; cowpeas thrive in summer heat.

directions: This productive bush-type cowpea produces plump, succulent peas with a distinctive purple eye inside richly colored purple pods. Sow directly into warm soil, as cowpeas love summer heat and tolerate dry conditions. The compact plants need no staking and set a heavy crop of easy-to-shell pods. Pick pods when the hulls turn purple and the peas inside are full for fresh use, or leave them to dry for storage.

planting.note: Sow in spring or late summer; flavor sweetens after frost.

directions: Also known as dinosaur or Tuscan kale, this heirloom features deep blue-green, bumpy-textured leaves with rich, mild flavor. Sow directly in spring or late summer, as it is a cold-hardy crop whose flavor sweetens after a frost. Thin plants to give the tall, upright rosettes room to develop. Harvest outer leaves from the bottom up, letting the center keep producing for a long, continuous cutting season.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm, rich soil.

directions: This heirloom is grown for its unique hulless seeds, which are soft-shelled pepitas that need no peeling for roasting or snacking. Sow directly into warm, rich soil after frost, giving the vines plenty of room to spread. Keep moisture and nutrients steady as fruit develops. Harvest the mature pumpkins, then scoop and dry the green hulless seeds for a homegrown supply of pepitas.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost once soil warms.

directions: This classic indeterminate variety yields abundant clusters of bright red cherry tomatoes with fresh, zesty flavor all summer. Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant once the soil warms. Stake or cage the vigorous vines and remove suckers if you want to manage the sprawl. Pick tomatoes as they ripen to keep the plants setting new fruit continuously through the season.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost, or start indoors briefly.

directions: This heirloom produces small, round cucumbers with bright yellow skin and a clean, mild, crisp taste despite the citrusy name. Sow directly into warm soil after frost, or give it a short indoor start in cool climates. Trellis the vines to keep the round fruits clean and easy to spot. Harvest when the skin turns pale yellow and the cucumbers are about the size of an egg for the best flavor and texture.

planting.note: Direct sow; ideal for containers and shallow beds.

directions: This heirloom baby carrot produces small, vibrant orange roots packed with sweet flavor, bred originally for canning but excellent fresh or in containers. Sow seeds directly into loose soil and keep the bed evenly moist during the slow germination. Thin seedlings so the short roots can size up cleanly. Harvest while the roots are still small and tender for the sweetest, most delicate flavor.

planting.note: Sow in spring or late summer; heat causes bolting and bitterness.

directions: This Italian heirloom loose-leaf lettuce forms frilly rosettes of magenta-red and green leaves with mild flavor and striking color. Sow directly in cool weather, spring or late summer, since heat prompts bolting and bitterness. Sow in succession for a continuous supply of tender leaves. Harvest outer leaves as a cut-and-come-again crop, or cut whole rosettes when full, keeping soil evenly moist throughout.

planting.note: Time so the long crop matures into cool fall weather; flavor sweetens after frost.

directions: This time-tested heirloom from the late 1800s is valued for compact plants, dependable yields, and excellent cold tolerance. Start seeds indoors and transplant so the long-season crop matures into cool fall weather, when the sprouts taste sweetest after light frosts. Keep plants well watered and side-dress with nitrogen for steady growth. Harvest the firm sprouts from the bottom of the stalk upward as they reach about an inch across.

planting.note: Start indoors early; transplant only into warm soil with warm nights.

directions: This prolific Italian-style heirloom produces slender, club-shaped fruits with glossy purple skin and tender white flesh, up to about four per plant. Start seeds indoors eight weeks before the last frost, since eggplant needs a long, warm season. Transplant into warm soil once nights stay reliably above 55 degrees. Harvest the fruits while the skin is firm and glossy for the best mild flavor and texture.

planting.note: Start indoors or direct sow in spring; a long-lived perennial that returns yearly.

directions: Lovage is a bold, long-lived perennial herb with a strong savory flavor like an intense celery, with hints of anise and cumin. Start seeds indoors or sow directly in spring, giving this large plant plenty of room, as it can reach several feet tall. It prefers full sun to partial shade and rich, evenly moist soil. Harvest leaves and stems through the season for soups and stocks, and cut plants back to encourage fresh growth.

planting.note: Needs a long, hot season; start indoors and transplant onto a strong trellis.

directions: This vigorous climbing gourd serves two purposes: harvest young fruits as a tender, okra-like vegetable, or let them mature and dry into natural loofah sponges. It needs a long, hot season, so start seeds indoors and transplant onto a sturdy trellis once soil is warm. The heavy vines produce long, hanging fruits that require strong support. For sponges, leave the gourds on the vine until the skin browns and the interior fibers dry, then peel, shake out the seeds, and rinse.

planting.note: Start indoors early; transplant into warm soil after frost with warm nights.

directions: This classic Italian heirloom produces long, slender six-to-eight-inch peppers with thin skin, tender flesh, and sweet mild flavor, excellent roasted or grilled. Start seeds indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and transplant once nights stay warm. Support the plants as the heavy fruits develop. Harvest green for a milder taste, or let the peppers ripen to deep red for their fullest sweetness.
